chunk_summary: This section discusses trace element supplementation and toxicity risks in pediatric cholestasis and parenteral nutrition, focusing on zinc, selenium, copper, and manganese management to prevent deficiencies and toxicity.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and management of zinc and selenium deficiencies in pediatric cholestasis.
Learn the risks of manganese toxicity and strategies to minimize exposure in prolonged parenteral nutrition.
Identify appropriate supplementation dosages and monitoring protocols for trace elements in cholestasis.
Recognize the role of impaired biliary excretion in trace element toxicity.
key_facts: Zinc deficiency may present with poor growth, periorificial dermatitis, dysgeusia, or recurrent infections; a common dose is 1 mg/kg/day of elemental zinc with reassessment in 4–8 weeks.
Selenium deficiency can cause cardiomyopathy, myopathy, and immune dysfunction, especially in prolonged parenteral nutrition.
Manganese toxicity, linked to impaired biliary excretion, can cause neurotoxicity; whole-blood manganese monitoring is recommended when available.
Copper and manganese require careful balance due to the risk of toxicity in cholestasis.
Serum zinc is an inflammation-sensitive marker and may not always reflect true deficiency.
reference_quote: Deficiency can worsen appetite, immune function, wound healing, and neurodevelopment, while impaired biliary excretion can increase toxicity risk for selected elements. (Source: NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN joint position paper)
document_key_topics: Trace element supplementation in pediatric cholestasis
Monitoring and dosing guidelines for trace elements
